title: Settings management vs complete complex form vs toggle switch vs progressive disclosure
summary: Use settings management for durable user or app configuration, complete complex form for one large dependency-checked edit surface, toggle switch for one immediate binary setting, and progressive disclosure for optional complexity inside another task.
decisionRules: Choose settings management when users need a revisitable place to inspect, search, group, change, save, reset, and recover persistent app, account, workspace, privacy, notification, display, integration, or device configuration.
Choose complete complex form when one coherent configuration task has many related fields and cross-section dependencies that must be validated or reviewed before a single save.
Choose toggle switch when the task is one immediate, reversible binary setting with clear on/off state and no wider settings navigation or grouping requirement.
Choose progressive disclosure when optional advanced controls belong inside a primary task and should be revealed only after intent, not when users need a durable settings center.
Choose inline edit when users change one visible value in place and do not need settings categories, search, reset defaults, scope labels, or save/discard policy.
Choose profile setup when the settings-like work is specifically completing an existing profile's readiness, visibility, or managed fields rather than managing app behavior.
Settings management must label scope such as device, account, workspace, organization, integration, or system before users change values.
Settings management must distinguish immediate apply from Save, Apply, restart, sync, inherited, managed, and approval-gated settings.
Settings management may contain toggles, selects, radios, links, and forms, but the pattern is the durable organization, current-value inspection, status, and recovery contract around those controls.

Do not use settings management as a dumping ground for unrelated navigation, billing history, onboarding tasks, destructive account deletion, support content, or profile completion unless those areas have explicit settings ownership.
failureModes: A page labelled Settings mixes unrelated destinations and commands without scope, grouping, or current values.
One row applies immediately, another requires Save, and a third requires restart, but the page uses the same visual treatment for all three.
A workspace-wide setting is changed from a local project page without warning users about the broader scope.
A complete complex form is split into scattered settings pages, hiding dependencies that must be reviewed together.
A single toggle opens a full settings center even though the user only needs one reversible on/off choice.
Advanced task options are moved to global Settings, so users cannot understand how they affect the current task.

sources.note: Apple supports settings as configuration users can adjust, with placement decisions based on change frequency.
Material supports settings as app behavior preferences with Settings navigation, labels, secondary text, switches, checkboxes, and status.
Android supports preference hierarchy, current values, dependencies, and persisted settings.
Microsoft supports dedicated settings pages and recommendations for creating and displaying app settings.
